text: Welcome to This is My Architecture. Today I have here Kevin from Karim. Hi Kevin. Hello Cameron.
Can you tell me a bit more about Karim? Yeah so Karim started in 2012 as a ride-hailing
company and then over the next eight years we grew to be one of the region's most successful
startups. That led us to be acquired by Uber last year and now we're expanding into an
array of different services such as food deliveries, grocery deliveries, P2P transactions,
payments and that's in an attempt to become the region's leading super app. I definitely
love to use Karim. So can you tell me, so we have a very interesting architecture here,
can you tell me the use case that you're looking at? Yes so essentially we
wanted to build a fraud detection system that covers all of Karim. So traditionally
we'd see a fraud pattern and then we'd build a project or a model specifically for that one
pattern whereas now we want like a blanket approach to cover all different types of fraud.
So can you give me an example of a fraud that happens? Yes so one of the most common
ones is users gaining access to stolen credit card details and then using that credit card
to make purchases, book rides, do anything on the platforms. So let's dive in. Can you explain
me how this how this works? Yes so the entire project is what we call Crazy Wall and so initially
for the project we need a database that is populated with all of our historical data about our users.
So everything you see above this line here is all the historical data. So we have our data warehouse
hosting hosted on S3 running on Hive and we then have an EMR cluster that extracts all of the data
and it uses PySpark to run an ETL pipeline which extracts transforms and loads the data in the format
that's needed by the Neptune database. And so at the moment once we've done all that we have a graph
database that's a historical snapshot of all our users. That's great. So why a graph database? So the
idea behind the project is to have a representation of the identity of all of our users. So graph is a
perfect way to do that where we have users as nodes and all of their data points on their profile as
nodes as well. And then users who share different data points they'll be represented as edges in the
graph. So the data points like credit card details and those kind of things are all the edges. Yeah
exactly. So I know you have a few choices when you choose a graph database. Why Neptune? Yeah so we
compared quite a number of different frameworks and we ended up choosing Neptune firstly because as
you can see we have a lot of different services already hosted on AWS and secondly Neptune is fully
managed by AWS so we don't have to worry about scalability, maintenance, backups, anything like
that. And focus on your use case actually. So we looked into historical data. What about real-time events?
Yeah so everything then you see below the line is the process in real-time. So we have a system
developed by our engineers known as Pi Event Processor and this essentially processes every event that users
perform on the app. So if they log in, if they book a ride, make a purchase, everything is processed
by the system. So as these events happen they're streamed in real-time through this SQS queue into
our core code base in Beanstalk and that data is then again processed and transformed and written to
the database. And then once that ride happens it triggers a second read as well and this read is
again our core code base extracting all the data about this user and their surrounding neighbors in the
graph. Once we've extracted that data we then have a simple set of rules that run in here to try to
decide whether that action performed by the user is likely to be fraudulent or not. Okay so what kind
of accuracy are you getting from this rule-based setup? Yes it's a very simple set of rules at the moment but
we're already hitting a precision of right about 90 percent. That's very impressive. Okay and what do you do with the other 10 percent?
So what happens once we we make a classification whether a user is fraudulent or not, the decision is
written to a database and then that decision can go one of two ways. So we have another project that measures
how valuable customers are to us. So if they're a high value customer they're added to a queue in a ticketing
system to be manually reviewed so it requires some manual intervention to see if that user should be blocked
or not. Otherwise if they're a lower value user they're just automatically blocked by the project.
Because you don't want your high value customer to be blocked in an automatic manner. Yeah exactly with 90
precision we don't want to be having 10 percent false positives of genuine users. And what kind of scale are
we talking about in terms of blocking? So to date since we launched the project we blocked about
35,000 users. So 35,000 bad actors. Yeah pretty much. So you have many divisions within the company. How do they
get benefit from this architecture that you've created? So we built this huge graph obviously with
the intention for a fraud detection project. But obviously once we realize we have all this this
wealth of data in the graph it could be used by all different terms teams across Kareem. So we expose
the graph through an API and this allows anyone in Kareem to then query the graph get the
details about a user and their identity in the graph. So this can be used by teams in Kareem for
downstream tasks such as building different machine learning models that use this data.
Otherwise another example is we have a UI which allows anyone to view a particular user in the
graph and see what they look like and who they're sharing data with. Yep okay so what's next for you
in terms of the architecture? So as I said we have a very simple rule-based approach here and the aim was
always to add more intelligence to the system to the use of machine learning. So what we're doing now for
version 2 of the project is we're replacing our rules with Neptune ML and this is a framework developed
by Neptune which allows you to train deep learning models on the graph. So we're training a graph
convolutional network on all the data and users in the graph and the idea here is we'll be able to more
accurately predict which users are fraudulent or not. So ideally we want to improve this 90% precision
closer to 100% and secondly we want to improve the recall which means we'll be increasing the number of
users we block as well and with this improved precision if we're if we get it high enough we'll
be confident enough to get rid of this manually review system and then the whole project will be
100% automated. That would be a great benefit for your company. So what is part of your wishlist from AWS?
Yeah so first of all as I explained we're integrating Neptune ML now and that was always the plan
but before Neptune released Neptune ML we we had the plan to build this full deep learning pipeline
completely in-house on our own which would be a huge amount of work. So now that Neptune has released
Neptune ML it takes a lot of the workload off of us and we can focus on building the most powerful model
possible and secondly a lot of our data in a graph you have properties on both nodes and edges and we
have a huge amount of properties on both the nodes and edges but currently most deep learning models only focus
on the node properties whereas now Neptune ML we're we're working with the Neptune ML team to develop
a new type of model that will also use all of these properties on edges that we have in the graph.
That makes a lot of sense. Kevin thank you for sharing your work with me today.
Thank you Cameron. And thank you for joining This is My Architecture.
